conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions. Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I have the most accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have two children - Alex, who is 10 years old, and Blake, who is 2 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have two children - Alex (10 years old) and Blake (2 years old).

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake, I need to ask you a few questions.

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't live in the UK at the moment. I'm living abroad.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since you're living abroad, there are specific circumstances where you may still be eligible for Child Benefit.

**Which country are you currently living in?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Living in Spain may affect your eligibility, but there are some circumstances where you might still qualify.

**Are you a Crown servant (for example, working for the UK government or diplomatic service abroad), or is your partner a Crown servant?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of us are Crown servants. We're not working for the UK government or diplomatic service.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. 

**Do you or your partner have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, we don't have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Based on what you've told me, I need to understand your situation a bit more. According to the guidance, you may be able to get Child Benefit if you go to live in certain countries, but the specific eligibility criteria depend on your circumstances.

**Are you or your partner still paying UK National Insurance contributions, or do you have any ongoing connection to the UK such as being temporarily posted abroad for work?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, we're not paying UK National Insurance contributions, and we don't have any ongoing connection to the UK. We've just moved here permanently.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex and Blake.

Let me make my assessment based on the official UK government guidance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit** for either Alex or Blake.

**For Alex (10 years old):**
While Alex meets the age requirement (under 16), you do not meet the residency requirement. Child Benefit requires that you normally live in the UK. Since you've moved permanently to Spain and are not a Crown servant, do not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and are not paying UK National Insurance contributions, you do not qualify for an exception to the UK residency rule.

**For Blake (2 years old):**
The same applies to Blake. Although Blake meets the age requirement (under 16), the residency requirement is not met for the same reasons as above.

**In summary:** The fundamental requirement for Child Benefit is that you must live in the UK. While there are some exceptions for people living abroad (such as Crown servants or those with certain status under the EU Settlement Scheme), you do not meet any of these exceptions. Therefore, unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for either of your children while living permanently in Spain.

If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if you return to live in the UK - you may be able to claim Child Benefit at that time.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 10 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 2 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 2 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for either child.
